title: Model Diagnostics and Comparison
text: use irregular_migration dataset shipped package. Two models fitted—Poisson Negative Binomial—year sex covariates alpha year covariates beta. Quick summaries:

title: Model Diagnostics and Comparison
text: compare_models() produces side--side table log-likelihood, AIC, BIC, deviance, Pearson chi-squared, RMSE, three pseudo R2R^2 measures: R2_cor: cor(m,μ̂)2\mathrm{cor}(m, \hat\mu)^2 — squared correlation observed fitted values. R2_D: Explained deviance 1−D(model)/D(null)1 - D(\text{model}) / D(\text{null}), null model specification without covariates (single α\alpha, single β\beta). Measures much covariates improve fit beyond baseline power-law structure. R2_CW: Cameron–Windmeijer (1996) pseudo R2R^2, uses model-implied variance function V(μ)V(\mu) — specifically designed count data regression. Models sorted AIC (default). Note R2_D compares fitted model null model (method, covariates). model cov_alpha / cov_beta, R2_D = 0 construction — becomes informative covariates present. Lower AIC BIC values indicate better trade-fit complexity. Pearson chi-squared statistic divided degrees freedom (approximately n_obs - n_par) gives quick dispersion check: values much greater 1 suggest overdispersion, favours NB model Poisson.
code: comp <- compare_models(Poisson = fit_po, NB = fit_nb, sort_by = "AIC") comp #> Model comparison #> ------------------------------------------------------------  #>    Model  Method Estimator  Link Constrained n_par   logLik      AIC      BIC #>       NB      NB       MLE power       FALSE    15 -2621.12  5272.23  5350.70 #>  Poisson POISSON       MLE power       FALSE    14 -8217.25 16462.49 16535.73 #>  Deviance Pearson_X2   RMSE R2_cor    R2_D  R2_CW #>   1275.97    3090.80 205.92 0.4058 -0.0030 0.9526 #>  13831.69   17500.68  52.53 0.8280  0.3139 0.9827

title: Model Diagnostics and Comparison
text: Poisson model nested within Negative Binomial (Poisson NB theta -> infinity), can use likelihood ratio test. lrtest() applies boundary correction Self & Liang (1987) automatically comparing Poisson NB, dispersion parameter lies boundary parameter space H0. small p-value indicates significant overdispersion, favouring NB model.
code: lrtest(fit_po, fit_nb) #> Likelihood ratio test #> ----------------------------------------  #> Model 1: POISSON   (logLik = -8217.25 ) #> Model 2: NB   (logLik = -2621.12 ) #> LR statistic: 11192.26 on 1 df #> (Boundary-corrected: 0.5 * P(chi2 > LR), Self & Liang 1987) #> p-value: < 2.2e-16

title: Model Diagnostics and Comparison
text: residuals() method supports four types: response: raw residuals, mi−μ̂im_i - \hat\mu_i. pearson: standardised variance function, (mi−μ̂)/V(μ̂)(m_i - \hat\mu_i) / \sqrt{V(\hat\mu_i)}. deviance: signed square root individual deviance contributions. anscombe: variance-stabilising residuals (approximately standard normal well-specified model).
code: r_resp <- residuals(fit_nb, type = "response") r_pear <- residuals(fit_nb, type = "pearson") r_dev  <- residuals(fit_nb, type = "deviance") r_ansc <- residuals(fit_nb, type = "anscombe")  summary(r_pear) #>     Min.  1st Qu. title: Model Diagnostics and Comparison
text: plot() fitted model produces Zhang (2008) four-panel display: Fitted vs Observed (sqrt scale) – points cluster around 45-degree line. Anscombe residuals vs fitted – running mean flat near zero (systematic misfit). Scale-Location – absolute Anscombe residuals vs fitted; increasing trend signals -modelled variance. Normal Q-Q – Anscombe residuals follow reference line distributional assumption holds.  look : Curvature Panel 1 suggests mean function misspecified. non-flat smoother Panel 2 indicates systematic bias certain fitted-value ranges. fan shape Panel 3 points overdispersion heteroscedasticity. Heavy tails S-shapes Panel 4 indicate departure assumed distribution. Compare Poisson fit see whether switching NB resolves patterns:
code: op <- par(mfrow = c(2, 2)) plot(fit_nb, ask = FALSE) par(op) op <- par(mfrow = c(2, 2)) plot(fit_po, ask = FALSE) par(op)

title: Model Diagnostics and Comparison
text: Rootograms (Kleiber & Zeileis, 2016) compare observed fitted count-frequency distributions directly. Three display styles available: hanging (default): observed bars hung fitted curve. model fits well, bottom bar touches zero. standing: observed bars stand x-axis fitted curve overlaid. Harder judge discrepancies visually. suspended: plots difference fobs−fexp\sqrt{f_\text{obs}} - \sqrt{f_\text{exp}} directly. Bars crossing zero indicate - -prediction count.   Compare two: Poisson rootogram shows bars consistently hanging zero line small counts moderate counts, classic overdispersion signature NB model correct.
code: rootogram(fit_nb, style = "hanging") rootogram(fit_po, style = "hanging") rootogram(fit_nb, style = "standing") rootogram(fit_nb, style = "suspended")

text: Dropping rows country measures structural contribution. computationally intensive (one refit per country). print() method shows top 10 influential countries ranked |Δξ||\Delta\xi|, summary() reports coefficient xi stability across LOO refits. xi bar plot shows countries pull total estimate (red bars, dropping country decreases estimate) (blue bars).

title: Model Diagnostics and Comparison

Bottom-left (-, -): dropping unit decreases estimate models. unit pulling estimate . -diagonal (top-left bottom-right): divergent influence. unit affects two models opposite directions—sign model choice matters unit. title: iOLS: Iterated OLS for Gamma Pseudo-Maximum Likelihood
text: uncounted package supports five estimation methods power-law model E(mi)=Niαi(γ+ni/Ni)βiE(m_i) = N_i^{\alpha_i}(\gamma + n_i/N_i)^{\beta_i}. vignette describes iOLS (iterated Ordinary Least Squares) estimator, targets Gamma Pseudo-Maximum Likelihood (GPML) solution via sequence linear regressions. method proposed Benatia, Bellego Pape (2024) computationally simple alternative nonlinear GLM estimation. key advantage: iteration standard OLS regression, gradient Hessian computation needed.

title: iOLS: Iterated OLS for Gamma Pseudo-Maximum Likelihood
text: log scale model linear: logE(mi)=αilogNi+βilog(γ+ni/Ni)=𝐳i′𝛉\log E(m_i) = \alpha_i \log N_i + \beta_i \log(\gamma + n_i/N_i)               = \mathbf{z}_i'\boldsymbol{\theta} 𝐳i\mathbf{z}_i design vector 𝛉=(α,β)′\boldsymbol{\theta} = (\alpha, \beta)'. exponential conditional mean model E(mi|𝐳i)=exp(𝐳i′𝛉)E(m_i | \mathbf{z}_i) = \exp(\mathbf{z}_i'\boldsymbol{\theta}).

title: iOLS: Iterated OLS for Gamma Pseudo-Maximum Likelihood
text: two leading PML estimators model: Poisson PML (PPML) solves ∑(mi−μi)𝐳i=𝟎\sum_i (m_i - \mu_i)\mathbf{z}_i = \mathbf{0}. weights observations μi\mu_i information matrix, giving influence large-count observations. Gamma PML (GPML) solves ∑(mi/μi−1)𝐳i=𝟎\sum_i (m_i/\mu_i - 1)\mathbf{z}_i = \mathbf{0}. gives equal weight observations regardless μi\mu_i, Gamma variance function V(μ)=μ2V(\mu) = \mu^2 implies unit working weights log link. consistent 𝛉\boldsymbol{\theta} correct specification conditional mean. differ efficiency sensitivity outliers: PPML efficient data Poisson-like, GPML robust observations unusually large counts relative μ\mu.

title: iOLS: Iterated OLS for Gamma Pseudo-Maximum Likelihood
text: iOLS solves GPML score equations via two phases.

title: iOLS: Iterated OLS for Gamma Pseudo-Maximum Likelihood
text: Initialize 𝛉̂0\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_0 OLS log(m+1)\log(m+1). δ\delta increasing sequence (default: 1, 10, 100, 1000): Compute μ̂=exp(𝐳i′𝛉̂t)\hat{\mu}_i = \exp(\mathbf{z}_i'\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_t) Set ỹ=log(mi+δμ̂)−c‾\tilde{y}_i = \log(m_i + \delta\hat{\mu}_i) - \bar{c}, c‾\bar{c} weighted mean log(mi+δμ̂)\log(m_i + \delta\hat{\mu}_i) (empirical centering) Update 𝛉̂t+1\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{t+1} regressing ỹ\tilde{y} 𝐙\mathbf{Z} using OLS Repeat convergence phase guaranteed converge globally (contraction mapping modulus κ<1\kappa < 1). Larger δ\delta gives better approximation GPML slower convergence.

title: iOLS: Iterated OLS for Gamma Pseudo-Maximum Likelihood
text: Phase 1 converges, switch exact debiasing step: ỹ=logμ̂+mi/μ̂−11+ρ\tilde{y}_i = \log\hat{\mu}_i + \frac{m_i/\hat{\mu}_i - 1}{1 + \rho} ρ>0\rho > 0 stability parameter (default: 1). theoretically correct form Benatia et al. (2024) targets exact GPML score. convergence: ∑(mi/μ̂−1)𝐳i=𝟎\sum_i (m_i/\hat{\mu}_i - 1)\mathbf{z}_i = \mathbf{0} GPML first-order conditions.

title: Theoretical Framework for Hidden Population Estimation
text: Estimating size unauthorized (hidden) populations fundamental problem official statistics migration research. definition, populations avoid administrative registration, single data source provides complete count. However, multiple partial administrative sources available – example, border-guard apprehensions, police identifications, social-insurance records – statistical models can exploit cross-source variation infer size population escapes sources. uncounted package implements estimation framework Beresewicz Pawlukiewicz (2020), builds dual-system ideas Zhang (2008). core insight observed count unauthorized migrants given group (e.g., country--origin ×\times year cell) power-law function reference (known) population, modulated auxiliary registration rate. Fitting power-law model inverting yields estimate total hidden population. vignette describes theory behind model, available estimation methods, inference procedures implemented package.

title: Theoretical Framework for Hidden Population Estimation
text: Let =1,…,ni = 1, \ldots, n index observational units (e.g., country--origin ×\times year ×\times sex cells). Denote mim_i: observed count unauthorized migrants (e.g., apprehensions), NiN_i: reference population size (e.g., insured foreigners origin), nin_i: auxiliary count second administrative source (e.g., police identifications). conditional mean mim_i modelled E[mi∣Ni,ni]=ξiρi,ξi=Niα, E[m_i \mid N_i, n_i] \;=\; \xi_i \rho_i,   \qquad \xi_i = N_i^{\alpha}, α\alpha, β\beta, γ≥0\gamma \geq 0 parameters estimated. Define ηi=βlog(γ+niNi). \eta_i = \beta \log\!\left(\gamma + \frac{n_i}{N_i}\right). package supports three links ρi=h(ηi)\rho_i = h(\eta_i): link_rho = "power": ρi=exp(ηi)\rho_i = \exp(\eta_i), link_rho = "cloglog": ρi=1−exp(−exp(ηi))\rho_i = 1 - \exp(-\exp(\eta_i)), link_rho = "logistic": ρi=(1+exp(−ηi))−1\rho_i = (1 + \exp(-\eta_i))^{-1}. log scale, becomes logE[mi]=αlogNi+logρi. \log E[m_i] \;=\; \alpha \,\log N_i \;+\; \log \rho_i. every supported detection link, ξi=E[mi]ρi=Niα. \xi_i = \frac{E[m_i]}{\rho_i} = N_i^\alpha. public population-size estimator remains ξ̂=∑iNiα̂\hat\xi = \sum_i N_i^{\hat\alpha_i} even bounded detection link used. choice link_rho affects ξ̂\hat\xi indirectly fitted value α̂\hat\alpha.

title: Theoretical Framework for Hidden Population Estimation
text: α\alpha – elasticity respect reference population. parameter α\alpha governs expected observed count scales reference population. α=1\alpha = 1, relationship proportional; α∈(0,1)\alpha \(0, 1), scaling sublinear, meaning larger reference populations yield proportionally fewer detections. Values α\alpha near zero indicate weak dependence population size. constraint α∈(0,1)\alpha \(0, 1) theoretically motivated always imposed estimation (see Section “Covariates” ). β\beta – elasticity respect registration rate. parameter β\beta captures association auxiliary registration rate (γ+ni/Ni)(\gamma + n_i / N_i) observed unauthorized count. positive β\beta indicates groups higher auxiliary detection rates also tend observed unauthorized migrants. expected sources partially detect latent population. γ\gamma – offset zero auxiliary counts. Many groups may ni=0n_i = 0 (auxiliary detections), make log(ni/Ni)\log(n_i / N_i) undefined. offset γ≥0\gamma \geq 0 ensures rate term remains positive. practice, γ\gamma typically small (close zero). can fixed known value, estimated data, omitted entirely ni>0n_i > 0.

title: Theoretical Framework for Hidden Population Estimation
text: Given α̂\hat\alpha, total unauthorized population estimated ξ̂=∑=1nNiα̂. \hat\xi \;=\; \sum_{=1}^{n} N_i^{\hat\alpha}. α̂<1\hat\alpha < 1 Ni>1N_i > 1 ii, ξ̂<∑iNi\hat\xi < \sum_i N_i, reflecting fact fraction reference population unauthorized. population size ξ̂\hat\xi monotone increasing function α\alpha (Ni≥1N_i \geq 1), property exploited confidence interval construction.

title: Theoretical Framework for Hidden Population Estimation
text: uncounted package implements four estimation methods. share mean structure; differ objective function distributional assumptions.

title: Theoretical Framework for Hidden Population Estimation
text: Ordinary least squares applied log-linearized model: logmi=αlogNi+βlog(ni+γNi)+εi. \log m_i = \alpha \log N_i + \beta \log\!\left(\frac{n_i + \gamma}{N_i}\right) + \varepsilon_i. mi=0m_i = 0, response replaced log(mi+1)\log(m_i + 1). OLS fast transparent ignores count nature mim_i may biased heteroscedasticity (log-transformation changes conditional mean). γ\gamma estimated, profiled via grid search: candidate γ\gamma, OLS coefficients obtained closed form, value minimizing residual sum squares selected.

title: Theoretical Framework for Hidden Population Estimation
text: Nonlinear least squares minimizes ∑=1n(mi−Niα(ni+γNi)β)2 \sum_{=1}^n \left(m_i - N_i^{\alpha}   \left(\frac{n_i + \gamma}{N_i}\right)^{\!\beta}\right)^2 using Levenberg–Marquardt algorithm. avoids log-transformation bias OLS still treats mim_i continuous.

title: Theoretical Framework for Hidden Population Estimation
text: Poisson log-likelihood ℓ(𝛉)=∑=1n[milogμi−μi], \ell(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \sum_{=1}^{n}   \bigl[m_i \log \mu_i - \mu_i\bigr], μi=Niαi(γ+ni/Ni)βi\mu_i = N_i^{\alpha_i}\,(\gamma + n_i/N_i)^{\beta_i}. shown Santos Silva Tenreyro (2006), Poisson PML estimator consistent parameters conditional mean E[mi∣Ni,ni]E[m_i \mid N_i, n_i] even true distribution Poisson, provided mean correctly specified. makes PPML robust heteroscedasticity recommended default uncounted. Poisson Negative Binomial count models, package can also use estimator = "gmm" estimator = "el". cases per-observation score equations treated moment conditions fitted momentfit. model μi\mu_i unchanged; estimating criterion differs. moment-based count fits default robust covariance HC1 rather HC3, leverage-based HC2/HC3 adjustments available generically setting.

title: Theoretical Framework for Hidden Population Estimation
text: Negative Binomial extends Poisson adding dispersion parameter θ>0\theta > 0. log-likelihood $$ \ell(\boldsymbol{\theta}, \theta) = \sum_{=1}^{n} \Bigl[   \log\Gamma(m_i + \theta) - \log\Gamma(\theta) - \log(m_i!)   + \theta\log\theta - \theta\log(\theta + \mu_i)   + m_i\log\mu_i - m_i\log(\theta + \mu_i) \Bigr]. $$ accounts overdispersion (variance exceeding Poisson mean) can provide efficient estimates data truly overdispersed. parameter θ\theta jointly estimated alongside α\alpha, β\beta, γ\gamma.

title: Theoretical Framework for Hidden Population Estimation
text: iOLS estimator (Benatia, Bellego & Pape, 2024) targets Gamma Pseudo-Maximum Likelihood (GPML) score equations ∑(mi/μi−1)𝐳i=𝟎\sum_i (m_i / \mu_i - 1)\,\mathbf{z}_i = \mathbf{0} via two-phase iterated OLS algorithm. Unlike PPML, weights observations μi\mu_i, GPML gives equal weight observations. makes robust influential high-count observations. See vignette("iols") full algorithm comparison. Currently gamma = "estimate" supported iOLS; use fixed gamma Poisson fit.

title: Theoretical Framework for Hidden Population Estimation
text: offset parameter γ\gamma handled differently depending method: Poisson / NB: γ\gamma jointly optimized within likelihood using L-BFGS-B box constraints (gamma_bounds, default [10−10,0.5][10^{-10},\, 0.5]). OLS: γ\gamma profiled via grid search bounds, followed local optimization (optimize()). methods: γ\gamma can fixed known value (gamma = 0.01) omitted entirely (gamma = NULL), latter requiring ni>0n_i > 0 observations.

text: output includes three pseudo R2R^2 measures: R2_cor (squared correlation), R2_D (explained deviance relative null model without covariates, see Cameron & Trivedi, 2013), R2_CW (Cameron–Windmeijer, 1997, accounts model’s variance function). Note R2_D: null model specification (method, gamma) without cov_alpha / cov_beta — .e., single α\alpha single β\beta. model already covariates, model = null R2_D = 0 construction. R2_D becomes informative compare models covariates (e.g., cov_alpha = ~ year + sex) covariate-free baseline: tells much deviance explained group-varying parameters beyond basic power-law structure.

what: Why Poisson as the main model?
title: Complete Analysis Workflow
text: Despite NB often better AIC/BIC (reflecting genuine overdispersion), Poisson model recommended primary specification several reasons: Consistency. Poisson PML consistent conditional mean long E(mi|Xi)E(m_i | X_i) correctly specified, regardless true variance function. NB requires correct specification mean variance. Robustness. HC-robust standard errors correct overdispersion Poisson model without adding extra parameter must estimated. Stability. NB model’s joint estimation gamma theta can numerically fragile many observations zero. two-stage approach mitigates introduces conditioning step. NB results serve useful robustness check population size estimate.

what: Bootstrap Confidence Intervals for Population Size — bootstrap_popsize
title: Bootstrap Confidence Intervals for Population Size — bootstrap_popsize
text: Computes bootstrap confidence intervals estimated population size using fractional weighted bootstrap (FWB) Xu et al. (2020). Supports cluster bootstrap account within-country correlation across years sex.

title: Bootstrap Confidence Intervals for Population Size — bootstrap_popsize
code: bootstrap_popsize(   object,   R = 199,   cluster = NULL,   level = 0.95,   ci_type = c("perc", "bc"),   point_estimate = c("median", "plugin", "mean"),   seed = NULL,   verbose = TRUE,   by = NULL,   total = FALSE )

title: Bootstrap Confidence Intervals for Population Size — bootstrap_popsize
text: object "uncounted" object (fitted model). R Number bootstrap replications (default 199). cluster One-sided formula cluster variable (e.g., ~ country_code). provided, FWB weights generated cluster level (observations cluster get weight). NULL, observation-level FWB used. level Confidence level (default 0.95). ci_type Type bootstrap CI: "perc" (percentile) "bc" (bias-corrected percentile). Default "perc". point_estimate point estimate report: "median" (default, recommended) – bootstrap median, robust skewness; "plugin" – plug-\(\hat{\xi} = \sum N^{\hat{\alpha}}\); "mean" – bootstrap mean. seed Random seed reproducibility. verbose Print progress bar? Default TRUE. Optional formula stratified population size estimation (e.g., ~ year, ~ country). provided, bootstrap population sizes computed per -group instead per cov_alpha group. See popsize details. total Logical; TRUE multiple groups exist, include total row computed summing per-replicate group estimates taking quantiles. Default FALSE.

title: Bootstrap Confidence Intervals for Population Size — bootstrap_popsize
text: object class "uncounted_boot" components: t Matrix (R x n_groups): bootstrap population size estimates per group. row one replicate. t0 Numeric vector: plug-point estimates original fit. t0_bc Numeric vector: analytical bias-corrected point estimates popsize() (multiplicative lognormal unconstrained fits, Taylor approximation constrained fits). popsize Data frame columns: group, estimate, lower, upper, estimate uses chosen point_estimate type. popsize_full Data frame point estimate types: plugin, plugin_bc, boot_median, boot_mean, lower, upper. total List total across groups (plugin, plugin_bc, median, mean, lower, upper), NULL one group. R Number replications requested. ci_type CI type used ("perc" "bc"). point_estimate Point estimate type used. level Confidence level. n_converged Number bootstrap replications model converged. cluster Logical; whether cluster bootstrap used.

title: Bootstrap Confidence Intervals for Population Size — bootstrap_popsize
text: Fractional weighted bootstrap (FWB). Instead resampling rows, FWB generates random non-negative weights Dirichlet distribution refits model weighted likelihood (Xu et al., 2020). avoids duplicate-row issues discrete data better suited GLMs. fwb package provides implementation. Cluster bootstrap. cluster specified, observations within cluster (e.g., country) receive FWB weight. preserves within-cluster correlation structure (e.g., country observed across multiple years sex categories). Cluster bootstrap recommended whenever observations independent. bootstrap median point estimate. transformation \(\xi = \sum N_i^{\alpha}\) convex \(\alpha\) \(N > 1\), Jensen's inequality \(E[\hat{\xi}] \geq \xi\). bootstrap distribution \(\hat{\xi}\) therefore typically right-skewed, bootstrap mean overestimates \(\xi\). bootstrap median robust central-tendency summary less sensitive skewness, default point estimate. Percentile CI (ci_type = "perc"). interval \([\hat{\xi}^{*}_{\alpha/2}, \; \hat{\xi}^{*}_{1-\alpha/2}]\), .e., \(\alpha/2\) \(1-\alpha/2\) quantiles bootstrap distribution. Bias-corrected percentile CI (ci_type = "bc"). Adjusts quantile probabilities median bias. Let \(z_0 = \Phi^{-1}(\mathrm{Pr}(\hat{\xi}^* < \hat{\xi}_0))\). adjusted quantile probabilities \(p_L = \Phi(2z_0 + z_{\alpha/2})\) \(p_U = \Phi(2z_0 - z_{\alpha/2})\), yielding \([\hat{\xi}^{*}_{p_L}, \; \hat{\xi}^{*}_{p_U}]\). Total across groups. multiple groups exist, total computed summing per-replicate group estimates taking quantiles. correctly accounts -group correlation within bootstrap replicate, unlike summing marginal quantiles.

title: Bootstrap Confidence Intervals for Population Size — bootstrap_popsize
text: Xu, L., Gotwalt, C., Hong, Y., King, C. B., & Meeker, W. Q. (2020). Applications fractional-random-weight bootstrap. American Statistician, 74(4), 345–358.

title: Compare LOO Results from Two Models — compare_loo
text: Purpose. comparing two model specifications (e.g., Poisson vs NB, different covariate sets), useful check whether observations influential models, whether influence patterns diverge. Scatter plot interpretation (4 quadrants). scatter plot (type = "scatter") shows \(\%\Delta\xi\) Model 1 (x-axis) vs Model 2 (y-axis). four quadrants reveal: Top-right (+, +): dropping obs increases \(\hat{\xi}\) models (obs pulls estimate ). Bottom-left (-, -): dropping obs decreases \(\hat{\xi}\) models (obs pulls estimate ). Top-left (-, +): divergent influence – obs pulls estimate opposite directions across models. Bottom-right (+, -): top-left reversed. Points near diagonal similar influence models. Points far diagonal divergent influence warrant investigation. Bar plot. bar plot (type = "bar") shows top N influential observations side side models, ranked max(|pct_1|, |pct_2|).

title: Compare multiple uncounted models — compare_models
text: Method Estimation method (OLS, NLS, POISSON, NB). Constrained Whether model fitted parameter constraints. n_par Number estimated parameters. logLik Log-likelihood (true count models, Gaussian pseudo-log-likelihood OLS/NLS). AIC, BIC Akaike Bayesian information criteria. Deviance Model deviance (see deviance.uncounted). Pearson_X2 Pearson chi-squared statistic \(\sum(m_i - \hat\mu_i)^2 / V(\hat\mu_i)\). RMSE Root mean squared error response residuals. R2_cor Squared correlation observed fitted values: \(\mathrm{cor}(m, \hat\mu)^2\). R2_D Explained deviance pseudo \(R^2\): \(1 - D(\text{model}) / D(\text{null})\), null model specification without covariates (single \(\alpha\), single \(\beta\)). Poisson/NB. See Cameron Trivedi (2013). R2_CW Cameron–Windmeijer (1996) pseudo \(R^2\): \(1 - \sum (m_i - \hat\mu_i)^2 / V(\hat\mu_i) \big/     \sum (m_i - \bar m)^2 / V(\bar m)\). Uses model-implied variance function \(V(\mu)\). Poisson/NB. warning issued comparing OLS/NLS pseudo-log-likelihoods true count-model likelihoods, since AIC/BIC values comparable across families.

title: Deviance for uncounted models — deviance.uncounted
text: formula depends estimation method: Poisson: $$D = 2\sum_{}\bigl[m_i\log(m_i/\hat\mu_i) - (m_i - \hat\mu_i)\bigr]$$ convention \(0 \log 0 = 0\). Negative Binomial: $$D = 2\sum_{}\bigl[m_i\log(m_i/\hat\mu_i) -   (m_i+\theta)\log\bigl((m_i+\theta)/(\hat\mu_i+\theta)\bigr)\bigr]$$ OLS / NLS: $$D = \sum_{}(\log m_i - \log\hat\mu_i)^2$$ .e., residual sum squares log scale.

title: Estimate the Size of an Unauthorized Migrant Population — estimate_hidden_pop
text: Model specification. observation \(\), expected observed count \(m_i\) modelled : $$E(m_i) = \xi_i \rho_i,\qquad \xi_i = N_i^{\alpha_i}$$ \(N_i\) reference (total registered) population, \(n_i\) auxiliary count (e.g., new registrations), \(\gamma \ge 0\) intercept-like offset. Define \(\eta_i = \beta_i \log(\gamma_i + n_i / N_i)\). \(\rho_i\) linked \(\eta_i\) one : "power" \(\rho_i = \exp(\eta_i)\) "cloglog" \(\rho_i = 1 - \exp(-\exp(\eta_i))\) "logistic" \(\rho_i = 1 / (1 + \exp(-\eta_i))\) log scale, $$\log E(m_i) = \alpha_i \log N_i + \log(\rho_i)$$ every supported link_rho, \(\xi_i = E(m_i) / \rho_i = N_i^{\alpha_i}\). Parameter interpretation. \(\alpha\) Elasticity observed count respect reference population. \(\alpha < 1\), total (unobserved) population \(\xi = \sum_i N_i^\alpha\) smaller \(\sum_i N_i\), reflecting incomplete coverage. Values \(\alpha\) near 0 imply weak dependence population size; values near 1 imply near-proportional scaling. \(\beta\) Elasticity respect registration rate \(\gamma + n_i / N_i\). positive \(\beta\) means higher auxiliary rates associated observed unauthorized migrants. \(\gamma\) Baseline registration-rate offset, ensuring rate term positive even \(n_i = 0\). Typically small (close zero). gamma = "estimate", profiled (OLS) jointly optimized (MLE methods). Estimation methods. OLS Ordinary least squares log-linearized model. Fast transparent ignores count nature \(m_i\) may inefficient heteroscedasticity. Uses \(\log(m_i)\) response (\(\log(m_i + 1)\) zeros present). Note: \(\log(m_i + 1)\) transformation used, fitted values \(\exp(\hat{\mu})\) \(\hat{\mu}\) estimated shifted scale, response-scale residuals diagnostics approximate. NLS Nonlinear least squares original scale \(m_i = N_i^{\alpha} (\gamma + n_i/N_i)^{\beta} + \varepsilon_i\). Avoids log-transformation bias OLS still treats \(m_i\) continuous. Poisson Poisson pseudo-maximum likelihood (PPML). Consistent heteroscedasticity long conditional mean correctly specified (Santos Silva Tenreyro, 2006). Recommended default. NB Negative Binomial MLE. Adds dispersion parameter \(\theta\) accommodate overdispersion beyond Poisson allows. Standard errors regression coefficients computed conditional \(\hat{\theta}\), consistent approach used MASS::glm.nb. means coefficient SEs account uncertainty \(\theta\) estimation. method = "poisson" method = "nb", moment conditions can also fit estimator = "gmm" estimator = "el" using momentfit. Constrained vs. unconstrained estimation. constrained = FALSE (default), \(\alpha\) \(\beta\) estimated real line without restrictions. constrained = TRUE, link functions enforce parameter bounds: \(\alpha = \mathrm{logit}(\eta)\) \(\alpha \(0, 1)\), \(\beta = \exp(\zeta)\) \(\beta > 0\). Coefficients reported link (transformed) scale; use summary() alpha_values / beta_values elements returned object response-scale values. Constrained estimation available Poisson NB methods . Population size estimation. total unauthorized population estimated \(\hat{\xi} = \sum_i N_i^{\hat{\alpha}_i}\). See popsize bias correction confidence intervals.

title: Leave-One-Out Sensitivity Analysis — loo
text: Purpose. LOO sensitivity analysis assesses much observation (country) influences estimated population size. observation large \(|\Delta\xi|\) relative full-model \(\hat{\xi}\) "influential" may warrant closer inspection. = "obs" vs = "country". = "obs", row dropped individually (n refits). identifies individual data points drive estimate, useful detecting outliers data errors. = "country", rows belonging one country dropped simultaneously (one refit per country). measures country's overall contribution \(\hat{\xi}\) relevant assessing structural sensitivity: conclusion change country excluded? Interpretation dxi. \(\Delta\xi_i = \hat{\xi}_{(-)} - \hat{\xi}\): change total estimated population observation (country) \(\) removed. \(\Delta\xi_i < 0\): dropping \(\) decreases estimate (observation pulling estimate ). \(\Delta\xi_i > 0\): dropping \(\) increases estimate (observation pulling estimate ).

title: Likelihood ratio test for nested uncounted models — lrtest
text: object1 "uncounted" model (Poisson NB). object2 "uncounted" model (Poisson NB). function automatically identifies simpler model regardless argument order.

title: Likelihood ratio test for nested uncounted models — lrtest
text: object class "uncounted_lrtest" components statistic, df, p_value, boundary, model descriptions, log-likelihoods.

title: Likelihood ratio test for nested uncounted models — lrtest
text: $$LR = 2\bigl(\ell_2 - \ell_1\bigr)$$ \(\ell_1\) \(\ell_2\) maximised log-likelihoods simpler complex models, respectively. \(H_0\) statistic follows \(\chi^2\) distribution degrees freedom equal difference number estimated parameters. Boundary correction Poisson vs NB. comparing Poisson model (H0) Negative Binomial model (H1), dispersion parameter \(\theta\) lies boundary parameter space \(H_0\) (\(\theta \\infty\)). standard \(\chi^2\) approximation invalid setting. Following Self & Liang (1987), p-value corrected $$p = 0.5\,\Pr(\chi^2_1 > LR)$$ corresponds 50:50 mixture point mass zero \(\chi^2_1\). function intended comparing count models (Poisson NB). OLS/NLS models supported use pseudo-log-likelihood. models must fitted data (identical number observations).

title: Likelihood ratio test for nested uncounted models — lrtest
text: Self, S. G. Liang, K.-Y. (1987). Asymptotic properties maximum likelihood estimators likelihood ratio tests nonstandard conditions. Journal American Statistical Association, 82(398), 605–610.

title: Likelihood ratio test for nested uncounted models — lrtest
code: set.seed(42) df <- data.frame(   N = rep(1000, 30),   n = rpois(30, lambda = 50) ) df$m <- rpois(30, lambda = df$N^0.5 * (df$n / df$N)^0.8)  fit_po <- estimate_hidden_pop(data = df, observed = ~m, auxiliary = ~n,                               reference_pop = ~N, method = "poisson") #> Warning: Some alpha values < 0 (min = -1.28). Consider using constrained = TRUE. fit_nb <- estimate_hidden_pop(data = df, observed = ~m, auxiliary = ~n,                               reference_pop = ~N, method = "nb") #> Warning: Some alpha values < 0 (min = -1.244). Consider using constrained = TRUE.  # Test for overdispersion (Poisson vs NB) lrtest(fit_po, fit_nb) #> Likelihood ratio test #> ----------------------------------------  #> Model 1: POISSON   (logLik = -52.51 ) #> Model 2: NB   (logLik = -52.52 ) #> LR statistic: 0 on 1 df #> (Boundary-corrected: 0.5 * P(chi2 > LR), Self & Liang 1987) #> p-value: 0.5

title: Estimated Population Size — popsize

title: Estimated Population Size — popsize
text: object "uncounted" object. ... Additional arguments (ignored). Optional formula specifying grouping variables stratified population size estimation (e.g., ~ year, ~ country, ~ year + sex). variables must exist data used fit model. NULL (default), groups defined alpha covariate pattern cov_alpha. provided, fitted alpha values used summed -groups instead. analogous singleRcapture::stratifyPopsize(). level Confidence level intervals (default 0.95). bias_correction Logical; apply analytical bias correction? Default TRUE. Uses model-based variance (HC-robust) avoid overcorrection inflated leverage-driven standard errors. total Logical; TRUE multiple groups exist, compute delta-method total SE CI, stored attr(result, "total"). Default FALSE. Warning: panel data groups defined time periods (e.g., = ~ year), total sums population estimates across years. generally meaningful individuals may appear multiple years. total appropriate groups represent non-overlapping subpopulations (e.g., = ~ sex within single year).

title: Estimated Population Size — popsize
text: data frame columns: group Group label derived alpha covariates, "()" alpha covariates specified. estimate Plug-estimate \(\hat{\xi}_g = \sum N_i^{\hat{\alpha}_g}\). estimate_bc Bias-corrected estimate \(\hat{\xi}^{BC}_g\); NA bias_correction = FALSE. lower Lower bound (bias-corrected) confidence interval. upper Upper bound (bias-corrected) confidence interval. share_pct Group share percentage total \(\hat{\xi}\). multiple groups exist, attr(result, "total") contains total estimate, bias-corrected estimate, standard error, CI.

title: Estimated Population Size — popsize
text: Point estimate. group \(g\) reference populations \(N_1, \ldots, N_n\) estimated exponent \(\hat{\alpha}_g\), plug-population size $$\hat{\xi}_g = \sum_{=1}^{n} N_i^{\hat{\alpha}_g}.$$ Bias correction. \(\xi\) nonlinear function \(\hat{\alpha}\), plug-estimate biased upward (Jensen's inequality, \(E[N^{\hat{\alpha}}] \geq N^{E[\hat{\alpha}]}\) \(N > 1\)). second-order Taylor expansion \(h(\alpha) = \sum_i N_i^{\alpha}\) around \(\alpha_0\) gives approximate bias $$   \mathrm{Bias}(\hat{\xi}_g) \approx     \frac{1}{2} \sum_{=1}^{n} N_i^{\alpha_g} (\log N_i)^2 \;     \mathbf{x}_g' \mathbf{V} \mathbf{x}_g, $$ \(\mathbf{x}_g\) design vector group \(g\) \(\mathbf{V}\) variance-covariance matrix \(\hat{\alpha}\). unconstrained models, exact multiplicative correction used: $$   \hat{\xi}^{BC}_g = \sum_{=1}^{n} N_i^{\hat{\alpha}_g}     \exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{2} (\log N_i)^2 \mathbf{x}_g' \mathbf{V}     \mathbf{x}_g\right), $$ exact normality \(\hat{\alpha}\) always positive. constrained models subtractive Taylor correction used instead (logistic-normal integral closed form), bias can positive negative depending \(\alpha\). Model-based variance used bias correction rather HC-robust variance. Poisson NB count models means Fisher-style inverse information mean-model parameters, evaluated fitted coefficients. remains true coefficients obtained estimator = "gmm" estimator = "el": robust HC FWB covariance still used confidence intervals, bias correction uses Fisher-style model variance MLE case, evaluated non-MLE estimate. iOLS/GPML, model-based variance \((\mathbf{Z}'\mathbf{Z})^{-1}\) (Gamma Fisher information, dispersion scaling). constrained = TRUE, delta method accounts logit link: \(\mathrm{Var}(\alpha) = \mathrm{Var}(\eta) \cdot [\sigma'(\eta)]^2\) \(\sigma'(\eta) = \alpha(1-\alpha)\). Confidence intervals via delta method. Let \(\mathbf{g}_g = \partial \hat{\xi}_g / \partial \boldsymbol{\alpha}\) denote gradient plug-estimator respect alpha coefficients. unconstrained models, \(\mathbf{g}_g = \sum_i N_i^{\hat{\alpha}_i} \log(N_i)\mathbf{x}_i;\) constrained models expression multiplied derivative inverse-logit map. Using HC-robust covariance \(\mathbf{V}\), package computes $$   \widehat{\mathrm{Var}}(\hat{\xi}_g) =     \mathbf{g}_g^\top \mathbf{V}\mathbf{g}_g. $$ preserve positivity, subgroup interval reported log-normal scale: $$   \hat{\xi}_g \exp\!\left(     \pm z_{\alpha/2}     \frac{\widehat{\mathrm{se}}(\hat{\xi}_g)}{\hat{\xi}_g}   \right). $$ bias_correction = TRUE, lower upper bounds rescaled \(\hat{\xi}^{BC}_g / \hat{\xi}_g\), matching returned bias-corrected point estimate. Total across groups. multiple groups exist, total \(\hat{\xi} = \sum_g \hat{\xi}_g\) delta-method CI computed via gradient \(\nabla_\alpha \xi\) log-normal approximation positivity. stored attr(result, "total").

title: Estimated Population Size — popsize
code: # Simulate synthetic data for 5 countries, 3 years each set.seed(42) n_obs <- 15 sim_data <- data.frame(   country = rep(paste0("C", 1:5), each = 3),   year    = rep(2018:2020, 5),   N       = rpois(n_obs, lambda = 500000),   n       = rpois(n_obs, lambda = 1000),   m       = rpois(n_obs, lambda = 50) )  # Fit a Poisson model fit <- estimate_hidden_pop(   data = sim_data, observed = ~m, auxiliary = ~n,   reference_pop = ~N, method = "poisson" ) #> Warning: Some alpha values < 0 (min = -0.467). Consider using constrained = TRUE.  # Population size with bias correction and 95% CI popsize(fit) #>   group observed   estimate estimate_bc lower upper share_pct #> 1 (all)      752 0.03262174           0     0     0       100  # Without bias correction popsize(fit, bias_correction = FALSE) #>   group observed   estimate estimate_bc         lower         upper share_pct #> 1 (all)      752 0.03262174          NA 3.458853e-219 3.076679e+215       100  # 90% confidence interval popsize(fit, level = 0.90) #>   group observed   estimate estimate_bc lower upper share_pct #> 1 (all)      752 0.03262174           0     0     0       100

title: Residuals for uncounted models — residuals.uncounted
text: Four residual types supported: Response residuals $$r_i = m_i - \hat\mu_i$$ Pearson residuals $$r_i = \frac{m_i - \hat\mu_i}{\sqrt{V(\hat\mu_i)}}$$ variance function \(V(\mu)\) depends model: Poisson: \(V(\mu) = \mu\) Negative Binomial: \(V(\mu) = \mu + \mu^2/\theta\) OLS/NLS: \(V(\mu) = \mu^2 \hat\sigma^2\) (delta-method approximation log scale) Deviance residuals Signed square root individual deviance contributions: $$r_i = \mathrm{sign}(m_i - \hat\mu_i)\,\sqrt{d_i}$$ \(d_i\) \(\)-th term deviance sum (see deviance.uncounted). OLS/NLS models, standardised log-scale residuals returned instead. Anscombe residuals Variance-stabilising residuals.
code: For Poisson (McCullagh & Nelder, 1989): \deqn{r_i = \frac{3\bigl(m_i^{2/3} - \hat\mu_i^{2/3}\bigr)}       {2\,\hat\mu_i^{1/6}}}  For Negative Binomial (Hilbe, 2011): \deqn{r_i = \frac{3\bigl(m_i^{2/3} - \hat\mu_i^{2/3}\bigr)}       {2\,\hat\mu_i^{1/6}\,(1 + \hat\mu_i/\theta)^{1/6}}}  For OLS/NLS: standardised log-scale residuals.

title: Residuals for uncounted models — residuals.uncounted
text: McCullagh, P. Nelder, J. . (1989). Generalized Linear Models, 2nd ed. Chapman & Hall. Hilbe, J. M. (2011). Negative Binomial Regression, 2nd ed. Cambridge University Press. Pierce, D. . Schafer, D. W. (1986). Residuals generalized linear models. Journal American Statistical Association, 81(396), 977–986.

Population size estimates may be unreliable. Consider using constrained = TRUE or simplifying cov_alpha.  # Response residuals (default) head(residuals(fit)) #> [1] -1.9426446 -1.2439567 -0.1250438 -0.5329768 -1.4553729 -1.0807200  # Pearson residuals head(residuals(fit, type = "pearson")) #> [1] -1.3937879 -0.6038371 -0.1178902 -0.3348827 -0.6231062 -0.6157254  # Deviance and Anscombe residuals head(residuals(fit, type = "deviance")) #> [1] -1.9711137 -0.6376606 -0.1201818 -0.3477951 -0.6544409 -0.6583125 head(residuals(fit, type = "anscombe")) #> [1] -2.0906818 -0.6379992 -0.1201894 -0.3478822 -0.6547205 -0.6588482

Additional arguments passed barplot. style Character: "hanging" (default), "standing", "suspended". max_count Maximum count value display. title: Rootogram for count model diagnostics — rootogram

title: Rootogram for count model diagnostics — rootogram
text: hanging (default) Bars representing observed frequencies "hung" fitted curve. bottom bar touch zero line fit good. Deviations zero indicate underfitting; zero, overfitting count. standing Observed bars stand x-axis fitted curve overlaid. Harder judge discrepancies hanging style eye must compare bar height curve. suspended Plots difference \(\sqrt{f_{\mathrm{obs}}} - \sqrt{f_{\mathrm{exp}}}\) directly. Bars cross zero indicate counts model - -predicts. default y-axis square-root scale (sqrt_scale = TRUE), stabilises visual variability bars makes easier judge fit tails.

title: Rootogram for count model diagnostics — rootogram
text: Kleiber, C. Zeileis, . (2016). Visualizing Count Data Regressions Using Rootograms. American Statistician, 70(3), 296–303.

Model Fitting Specify fit multiple models different methods (Poisson, NB, OLS, NLS) covariate structures. View coefficient summaries. Evaluate log-likelihood population size grid values chosen alpha beta coefficient. reoptimize = FALSE (default) holds parameters MLE (concentrated profile); reoptimize = TRUE re-optimizes nuisance parameters grid point (true profile). produce two-panel diagnostic plots. profile.uncounted() S3 method: Dispatches stats::profile() profile_gamma(), profile_alpha(), profile_beta() via param argument. Usage: profile(fit, param = "alpha"). bootstrap_popsize() per-replicate parameters: New $boot_params field output — R x n_params matrix storing coefficient estimates (plus gamma theta applicable) bootstrap replicate. title: uncounted 1.0.0

Available method = "poisson" method = "nb". Gamma coefficients estimated log scale jointly alpha, beta, theta. summary() displays response-scale gamma values delta-method standard errors per covariate group. predict(), tidy(), loo(), bootstrap_popsize() support new parameter.

title: uncounted 1.0.0
text: popsize() NA crash loo(): LOO drops observation makes refitted model unstable (e.g., rank-deficient design matrix), popsize() receive NA coefficients. Bare > 0 comparisons now use isTRUE() handle NA values safely. loo() loop also wraps popsize() tryCatch() individual failed iterations skipped rather killing entire analysis. tidy() gamma standard error: scalar gamma row now reports correct response-scale SE via delta method (SE(gamma) = gamma * SE(log gamma)). Wald statistic p-value set NA natural null hypothesis positive nuisance parameter. Confidence intervals use log-scale transformation (gamma * exp(+/- z * SE_log)) ensuring positivity. lrtest() gamma nesting: Now validates gamma submodel nesting via column-space inclusion models use cov_gamma. Also correctly detects fixed-gamma model nested cov_gamma design without intercept (e.g., ~ 0 + z). profile_gamma() cov_gamma fits: Now stops clear error instead silently profiling scalar gamma grid varying-gamma model. Shiny diagnostics tab also handles case gracefully.

title: uncounted 1.0.0
text: Version bump 1.0.0 reflecting new cov_gamma feature stabilized API. 52 new tests cov_gamma including simulation-based coefficient recovery, one-column formula edge cases, lrtest() nesting validation, profile_gamma() error handling. 650 tests total. .count_params() updated count multi-parameter gamma correct AIC/BIC.

title: uncounted 0.7.3
text: predict() factor encoding: Now uses training data’s terms object xlev consistent dummy encoding newdata. Reordered factor levels single-level subsets produce correct predictions. tidy() confidence intervals: OLS/NLS models now use qt() (matching t-based p-values) instead qnorm() confidence intervals.

title: uncounted 0.7.2
text: plot(popsize(fit)): New S3 plot method population size estimates. type = "estimate" (default) shows bias-corrected estimates CIs; type = "compare" shows plug-bias-corrected side side. compare_popsize(fit1, fit2, ...): Compare population size estimates across models, print plot methods. Supports parameter grouped comparison custom labels. predict(fit, newdata): Prediction method supporting new data, type = "response" (counts) type = "link" (log scale). Enables compatibility marginaleffects package. tidy(fit) glance(fit): broom-compatible methods enabling modelsummary::modelsummary() side--side model comparison tables. Requires generics package (Suggests).

title: uncounted 0.7.1
text: Multiplicative bias correction: Replaced subtractive Taylor approximation xi_BC = xi - bias exact multiplicative lognormal form xi_BC = sum N^alpha * exp(-0.5 * (log N)^2 * x'Vx). exact normality alpha-hat, always positive, reduces bias ~94% Monte Carlo simulations (vs ~88% old subtractive form). Constrained BC direction: logit constraint, second derivative h''(eta) can negative alpha near 1, bias correction can go either direction. code longer assumes upward bias constrained models. iOLS model-based variance: Uses (Z'Z)^{-1} (GPML Fisher information) without sigma^2 scaling, preventing inflated bias correction zero-heavy data. Removed nonexistent reference: Replaced Beresewicz, Gudaszewski & Walsh correct Zhang (2008) Beresewicz & Pawlukiewicz (2020) references.

title: uncounted 0.7.1
text: Extended iOLS tests: 39 new tests covering bootstrap, LOO, dfbeta, cluster-robust SEs, HC types, year*ukr migration specification, iOLS vs Poisson numerical comparisons. 493 tests total.

title: uncounted 0.7.0
text: iOLS estimator (method = "iols"): Iterated OLS targeting Gamma PML (GPML) score equations, following Benatia, Bellego & Pape (2024). Two-phase algorithm: Phase 1 warms increasing delta empirical centering; Phase 2 uses exact limiting transform y_tilde = log(mu) + (m/mu - 1)/(1+rho) solve Z'(m/mu - 1) = 0. Converges complex specifications including year * UKR interactions 50% zeros. Supports HC0-HC5 sandwich SEs via GPML score residuals. Currently requires fixed gamma (gamma = "estimate" yet supported). LOO rank-deficiency detection: loo(= "country") now checks rank-deficient design matrices refitting. dropping country removes covariate level (e.g., Ukraine year * UKR), refit skipped marked converged = FALSE warning.

title: uncounted 0.7.0
text: lrtest() nesting checks: Uses column-space inclusion via QR projection detect non-nested model pairs. Also warns cross-method comparisons (except Poisson vs NB), different constrained settings, incompatible gamma specifications. compare_models() warns mixed likelihood types: Now includes iOLS pseudo-loglik warning alongside OLS/NLS, preventing silent comparison GPML deviance Poisson/NB count likelihoods. Test coverage improvements: Added tests HC4/HC4m/HC5, LOO summary() plot(type = "coef"), bootstrap point_estimate/total/parameters, constrained summary output, profile_gamma(plot = TRUE). 440 tests total.

title: uncounted 0.6.0
text: Total CI printer: .print_popsize_table() now uses delta-method total CI attr(ps, "total") instead naively summing subgroup confidence bounds, ignored -group correlation. Constrained bias correction: second-order Taylor bias correction constrained models now includes logit curvature term alpha''(eta) = alpha(1-alpha)(1-2*alpha). Previously [alpha'(eta)]^2 used, producing incomplete bias correction alpha far 0.5. lrtest() nesting check: Now uses column-space inclusion (QR projection) detect non-nested model pairs, replacing previous heuristic. Also checks gamma specification compatibility.

title: uncounted 0.6.0
text: NB sandwich theta: Negative Binomial estimator now includes theta (dispersion) sandwich variance-covariance computation via dedicated NB covariance path using full per-observation score vector observed Hessian optim(hessian = TRUE). properly accounts theta estimation uncertainty coefficient standard errors. fitted object now includes theta_se, score_full, hessian_nll. Supports HC0, HC1, cluster-robust variance; HC2+ falls back HC1 message. CI workflows: Added GitHub Actions R-CMD-check (5 platforms), CRAN-like check, test coverage (Codecov), pkgdown site deployment. Unit test redesign: 340+ tests using shared 200-row simulated fixture, split CRAN (fast) extended (GitHub-) suites. Includes base R oracle tests glm(), lm(), sandwich::vcovHC(), MASS::glm.nb().

title: uncounted 0.5.0
